title: Phase transitions through excited-state level crossings and topological indicators: the case of the XXZ chain with staggered Ising interaction
authors: B. F. Márquez, K. Hallberg, A. A. Aligia
abstract: We combine two ways of determining the phase diagram of the spin-$1/2$ XXZ chain with a staggered Ising interaction and uniform transverse exchange, based on exact diagonalization. The model realizes a competition between Néel order and bond-dimerized phases generated by the alternating Ising interaction. The simplest approach to determine the phase boundaries is to use topological indicators based on generalized position operators. We show that in general, the bosonized and numerical results for the topological indicators agree. The second is based on crossings of excited energy levels and justified by conformal-field theory. In spite of the partial loss of translational symmetry caused by the alternating Ising interaction, we find that the latter method provides an accurate determination of the boundary between Néel and dimerized phases. Instead, while the jumps of a topological indicator provide a qualitatively correct phase diagram, its accuracy is affected when the gap is very small (or the correlation length very large) at one side of the transition, as we show using field-theoretical arguments. The combination of both methods provides a more efficient way of calculating phase diagrams for correlated one-dimensional models than other widely used conventional approaches.
summary: This work determines the phase diagram of a staggered XXZ spin chain by comparing two advanced diagnostic techniques: topological indicators and energy level crossings. The authors find that while both methods are powerful, the energy level crossing method provides a more reliable determination of the Néel-dimer transition boundary, especially when the system exhibits broken translational symmetry.
main_problem: The paper aims to determine the phase diagram, specifically the transition between Néel and dimerized phases, in the spin-1/2 XXZ chain subjected to a staggered Ising interaction.
main_result: The combination of two diagnostic methods—topological indicators and energy level crossings—provides a robust and efficient way to locate the phase boundary, with the level crossing method being particularly accurate despite the loss of translational symmetry.
method: The authors employ exact diagonalization on finite systems, comparing results from topological indicators (based on generalized position operators) with those derived from the crossing of excited energy levels (MCEL), which is justified by conformal-field theory.
model_or_system:
The system is a spin-1/2 XXZ chain Hamiltonian incorporating a staggered Ising interaction ($\delta 
eq 0$) and a uniform transverse exchange. This alternating interaction doubles the unit cell and drives the competition between Néel and bond-dimerized orders.
key_observables: Topological indicators ($\alpha_s$, $\alpha_\uparrow$), the pseudo-critical point $\delta_c(L)$ extracted from energy level crossings ($E^{(1)}_+(L, \delta_c) = E^{(1)}_-(L, \delta_c)$), and the correlation length ($\xi$).
important_parameters:
Anisotropy parameter ($\Delta$), staggering parameter ($\delta$), and system size ($L$). The analysis distinguishes between the homogeneous ($\delta=0$) and staggered ($\delta 
eq 0$) regimes.
main_assumptions: The topological indicator method's accuracy is compromised when the energy gap is very small near the transition. The analysis relies on mapping the low-energy physics to the double sine-Gordon theory and using finite-size scaling extrapolation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the extraction of the pseudo-critical point from the crossing of first excited energy levels for finite systems, and show the finite-size scaling of this critical point across different anisotropy values.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares two diagnostic tools—topological indicators and MCEL—to map the phase diagram. It analyzes the underlying physics using bosonization and relates the finite-size corrections to known critical theories (Ising universality class).
why_interesting: The rigorous comparison of multiple theoretical diagnostics (topological vs. spectral) to pinpoint a quantum critical point in a complex, interacting 1D model is highly valuable for understanding the robustness of phase transition detection in strongly correlated systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Excitation energies of low-lying states in the even (Sz tot, k′, F, P) = (0, 0, +, +) and odd (Sz tot, k, F, P) = (0, 0, −, −) sectors for L = 18 and ∆= 1.3, measured with respect to the ground state. The lowest odd-sector state E(0) − does not participate in the crossing associated with the N´eel- dimer transition. The finite-size pseudo-critical point is ex- tracted from the crossing between E(1) + and E(1) −.
FIG. 2. Finite size scaling of the pseudo-critical point ob- tained from the MCEL method for 1.4 ≤∆≤1.8. The extrapolated values δc(∞) were obtained by fitting Eq.(11) to linear order in L−1.

FIG. 3. Phase diagram obtained from exact diagonaliza- tion. Red triangles denote the thermodynamic extrapolation of the level crossing E(1) + = E(1) −, while blue dots denote the extrapolated sign change of the generalized position operator. Green squares are the DMRG data of Ref. [21], provided by H. Nigam. We use Ja = ∆(1 + δ) and Jb = ∆(1 −δ) to com- pare directly with Ref. [21]. The agreement between the red and green points supports the identification of the selected crossing as the finite-size precursor of the N´eel-dimer critical line.
